title: Inequalities Are Ranges
module: Week 1 - Linear Equations, Inequalities, and Functions
learningTarget: I can solve and graph inequalities and explain what the solution range means.
whyItMatters: An equation usually gives one answer. An inequality gives a range of answers, which is exactly how many real limits work: minimum age, maximum cost, at least this many credits, fewer than this many absences.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.studentText: A student has $30 for lunch this week. Each lunch costs $6. The inequality 6d <= 30 represents how many days the student can buy lunch.

questions.prompt: What does d <= 5 mean in this situation?

options.text: The student must buy lunch exactly 5 days.
The student can buy lunch 5 days or fewer.
The student can buy lunch more than 5 days.

questions.feedback: <= means the boundary value counts, and anything below it also works.

sections.title: Watch: Solving and graphing linear inequalities
sections.watchFor: Watch how the graph shows whether the boundary number is included.

contextQuestions.prompt: For y > 5, should the point at 5 be open or closed?
contextQuestions.feedback: The symbol > does not include the boundary value, so use an open point.

contextQuestions.prompt: In your own words, what does shading to the right on a number line usually mean?
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says the values are greater than the boundary.

sections.title: Boundary and Direction
sections.studentText: Graphing an inequality takes two decisions: whether the boundary number counts, and which direction the solutions go. Use a closed point for <= or >=. Use an open point for < or >.

steps: Put an open point at 13 because 13 is not included.
Shade to the right because the solutions are greater than 13.
workedExample.commonMistake: Students often solve correctly but shade the wrong direction. Test a number if you are unsure.

questions.prompt: A school event can spend no more than $250 on snacks. Write an inequality for buying x boxes at $12 each.

questions.feedback: >= includes -2 and all numbers greater than -2.

questions.prompt: Explain the difference between x < 6 and x <= 6.
questions.feedback: A strong answer says x < 6 does not include 6, while x <= 6 includes 6.

gate.completion: Student attempts the video questions, practice, and exit check.
gate.mastery: At least 80% on objective items and correct boundary/direction reasoning.
gate.remediation: Match five inequality symbols to open/closed point and shading direction.
gate.enrichment: Write and graph an inequality for a school policy or budget limit.
